title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: boundarylogic provides biplot-based post hoc explanation framework binary classification models. workflow three phases: Phase 1: Prepare data, fit model, project 2D biplot space, build prediction grid approximates decision boundary, visualise result. Phase 2: Global interpretations — find counterfactuals set observations, quantify variable importance, fit surrogate model explain biplot regions. Phase 3: Local interpretation single observation — find nearest counterfactual via biplot rotation, decompose move using Shapley values, produce sparse counterfactual changes variables possible. Phase 1 artifacts stored single bl_result object acts reproducible anchor Phases 2 3.

what: Option A: bl_prepare_data() — raw data frame
title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_prepare_data() converts raw data frame binary-class data frame produces reproducible train/test split. iris dataset three species. treat "versicolor" positive class (coded 1); species become 0. check: Features — four iris measurement columns selected automatically. Target — confirms 1/0 coding rule. Train rows / Test rows — close 80/20 150 = 120/30.
code: library(boundarylogic)  bl_dat <- bl_prepare_data(   data           = iris,   class_col      = "Species",   target_class   = "versicolor",   train_fraction = 0.8,   seed           = 121L )  print(bl_dat) #> <bl_data> #>   Features   : 4 (Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, Petal.Length, Petal.Width) #>   Target     : 'versicolor' -> 1, all others -> 0 #>   Train rows : 120  |  Test rows : 30 # Class balance in the training set table(bl_dat$train_data$class) #>  #>  0  1  #> 79 41 # First few rows head(bl_dat$train_data) #>   Sepal.Length Sepal.Width Petal.Length Petal.Width class #> 1          6.4         3.2          4.5         1.5     1 #> 2          4.8         3.4          1.6         0.2     0 #> 3          5.9         3.2          4.8         1.8     1 #> 4          4.6         3.1          1.5         0.2     0 #> 5          5.1         3.5          1.4         0.2     0 #> 6          7.0         3.2          4.7         1.4     1

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: data already split training test sets class column already coded numeric 0/1, use bl_wrap_data() bypass splitting conversion steps: bl_wrap_data() returns bl_data object bl_prepare_data(), downstream functions work identically.
code: # Example: data already prepared externally train_df <- my_train_data   # must have feature columns + a column named "class" (0/1) test_df  <- my_test_data    # same structure  bl_dat <- bl_wrap_data(   train_data   = train_df,   test_data    = test_df,   target_class = "versicolor"   # informational label only )  print(bl_dat)

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_filter_outliers() projects training data lightweight 2-PC space, builds convex hull enclosing hull_fraction points, removes observations outside hull, reports number percentage rows retained removed. Model fitting done separately Step 3. step optional — skip want use training data. check: Retained / Removed — rows percentages trimmed filter. Try hull_fraction = 1.0 keep points (filtering). Try hull_fraction = 0.8 aggressive trimming. Call bl_filter_outliers() multiple times different hull_fraction values find best setting, proceed Step 3 fit model retained data.
code: bl_filt <- bl_filter_outliers(   bl_data       = bl_dat,   hull_fraction = 0.91,   verbose       = TRUE ) #> Hull fraction: 0.91 | Retained: 110 (91.7%) | Removed: 10 (8.3%) print(bl_filt) #> <bl_filter_result> #>   Hull fraction : 0.91 #>   Retained      : 110 (91.7%) #>   Removed       : 10 (8.3%)

what: Option A: bl_fit_model() — built-in model types
title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_fit_model() fits chosen classifier (filtered) training data records training accuracy Gini coefficient. Note cutoff: cutoff = 0.5 selects decision boundary (probability threshold prediction switches class 0 class 1). default 0.5 need specified unless want investigate different threshold. cutoff = 0.5 gives valid boundary results current methodology. values accepted produce correct decision boundary estimates. Supported model types: tidymodels backends: model types except "GBM" fitted using tidymodels ecosystem (parsnip + workflows). fitted $model field workflows::workflow object, predictions use parsnip’s unified predict(<workflow>, type = "prob") interface. "GBM" uses gbm::gbm() directly gbm registered parsnip engine. Hyperparameter overrides via model_params: Parameter names follow parsnip conventions types except "GBM".
code: bl_mod <- bl_fit_model(   train_data = bl_filt$train_data,   var_names  = bl_filt$var_names,   model_type = "GLM",   # logistic regression   cutoff     = 0.5 )  print(bl_mod) #> <bl_model> #>   Model type    : GLM #>   Cutoff        : 0.5 #>   Train Accuracy: 0.7364  |  Train Gini: 0.6693 #>  #> --- Model summary --- #>  #> Call: #> stats::glm(formula = ..y ~ ., family = stats::binomial, data = data) #>  #> Coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    #> (Intercept)    7.3305     3.3929   2.161   0.0307 *  #> Sepal.Length  -0.2703     0.7447  -0.363   0.7166    #> Sepal.Width   -2.8850     1.0077  -2.863   0.0042 ** #> Petal.Length   1.7353     0.8202   2.116   0.0344 *  #> Petal.Width   -3.5300     1.4345  -2.461   0.0139 *  #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> (Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1) #>  #>     Null deviance: 144.21  on 109  degrees of freedom #> Residual deviance: 107.32  on 105  degrees of freedom #> AIC: 117.32 #>  #> Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 5 #>  #> --------------------- # GBM — uses gbm package parameter names directly bl_fit_model(train_data, var_names, model_type = "GBM",              model_params = list(n.trees = 1000, shrinkage = 0.05))  # NNET — parsnip parameter names bl_fit_model(train_data, var_names, model_type = "NNET",              model_params = list(hidden_units = 10, penalty = 0.01, epochs = 500))  # XGB — parsnip parameter names bl_fit_model(train_data, var_names, model_type = "XGB",              model_params = list(trees = 300, tree_depth = 5, learn_rate = 0.05))

what: Option B: bl_wrap_model() — your own model
title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: Use bl_wrap_model() fitted model externally — example, using complex hyperparameter tuning, caret/tidymodels workflow, model type natively supported. Wrapping model standard type (e.g., GLM custom settings): Wrapping fully custom model type: model uses non-standard predict() interface, supply predict_fn signature function(model, new_data) returning numeric probability vector: returned bl_model object fully compatible bl_build_result() bl_assemble().
code: # Fit the model yourself with any settings you need my_glm <- glm(class ~ ., data = bl_filt$train_data,               family = binomial(link = "logit"))  # Register it as a bl_model bl_mod <- bl_wrap_model(   model      = my_glm,   model_type = "GLM",                  # tells the package how to call predict()   var_names  = bl_filt$var_names,   train_data = bl_filt$train_data      # optional: compute accuracy/Gini )  print(bl_mod) # Example: a model fitted using a different package my_fn <- function(m, new_data) {   predict(m, new_data, type = "prob")[, "1"]  # extract class-1 column }  bl_mod <- bl_wrap_model(   model      = my_custom_model,   model_type = "custom",   var_names  = bl_filt$var_names,   predict_fn = my_fn,   train_data = bl_filt$train_data )

previous_headings: Phase 1 workflow > Steps 4–6 — Build and assemble
title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_build_result() combines projection, grid, assembly steps single call. Pass output bl_filter_outliers() (bl_prepare_data() filtering applied) fitted model; function handles everything else. Note: bl_build_result() always builds projection matrix V training data supplied object. need build V non-standard data source, use individual functions described Detailed route section . Use summary() detailed view including confusion matrix contour statistics: PCA vs CVA: PCA — separates observations overall variance. Use default starting point. CVA — maximises -class separation relative within-class spread. Supply bl_model use four-class confusion labels (TP/TN/FP/FN) automatically: Contour band width: rounding rounding parameter controls width band used extract decision boundary contour lines prediction grid: wider band (rounding = 2L) produces smoother, continuous contours robust model’s decision surface gradual. narrower band (rounding = 3L) traces boundary precisely. model predictions always rounded 3 decimal places regardless setting. Exploratory biplot (model yet): Omit bl_model get quick biplot data fitting. bl_build_result() returns bl_projection object can plotted directly:
code: bl_results <- bl_build_result(   bl_data  = bl_filt,     # pass bl_dat here if Step 2 was skipped   bl_model = bl_mod,   method   = "PCA",       # or "CVA" — see note below   m        = 100L,        # grid resolution; use 200L for final analysis   rounding = 3L           # contour band width: cutoff ± 0.001 )  print(bl_results) #> <bl_result> #>   Model type   : GLM #>   Method       : PCA #>   Features     : 4 (Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, Petal.Length, Petal.Width) #>   Train rows   : 110  |  Test rows: 30 #>   Cutoff       : 0.5 #>   Accuracy     : 0.7364  |  Gini: 0.6693 #>   Grid size    : 100 x 100 #>   Hull fraction: 1.00 #>   Created      : 2026-03-25 06:21:05 summary(bl_results) #> <bl_result> #>   Model type   : GLM #>   Method       : PCA #>   Features     : 4 (Sepal.Length, Sepal.Width, Petal.Length, Petal.Width) #>   Train rows   : 110  |  Test rows: 30 #>   Cutoff       : 0.5 #>   Accuracy     : 0.7364  |  Gini: 0.6693 #>   Grid size    : 100 x 100 #>   Hull fraction: 1.00 #>   Created      : 2026-03-25 06:21:05 #>  #> --- Model performance (training data) --- #> Confusion matrix (train): #>          Actual #> Predicted  0  1 #>         0 59 18 #>         1 11 22 #>  #> Grid probability range: [0.0060, 0.9030] #> Contour lines found  : 2 #> Polygon filter       : applied (fraction = 1.00) # CVA — confusion labels derived from bl_mod automatically bl_results <- bl_build_result(   bl_data  = bl_filt,   bl_model = bl_mod,   method   = "CVA" ) bl_proj_explore <- bl_build_result(bl_filt, method = "PCA") plot(bl_proj_explore)   # coloured by true class (red = class 1, blue = class 0)

previous_headings: Phase 1 workflow > Steps 4–6 — Build and assemble
title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: full control step, call three functions separately. useful want inspect intermediate outputs tune grid settings precisely. Step 4 — bl_build_projection() computes loading matrix V (inverse tV) maps observations p-dimensional feature space 2D biplot Z-space. Step 5 — bl_build_grid() creates m × m regular grid Z-space, back-projects point X-space, scores every grid point model, extracts decision boundary contour lines. Grid resolution guidance: check: Prob range — span values sides 0.5 meaningful boundary. Contour lines — ≥ 1; 0, model may predict single class across entire biplot plane. Step 6 — bl_assemble() collects Phase 1 outputs single bl_result object.
code: bl_proj <- bl_build_projection(   train_data  = bl_filt$train_data,   var_names   = bl_filt$var_names,   method      = "PCA",   standardise = TRUE,   proj_dims   = c(1L, 2L) )  print(bl_proj) bl_grid <- bl_build_grid(   train_data    = bl_filt$train_data,   bl_projection = bl_proj,   bl_model      = bl_mod,   m             = 100L    # use 200L for final analysis )  print(bl_grid) bl_results <- bl_assemble(   bl_data       = bl_filt,   # pass bl_dat if Step 2 was skipped   bl_model      = bl_mod,   bl_projection = bl_proj,   bl_grid       = bl_grid )  print(bl_results)

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: plot_biplotEZ() takes assembled bl_result draws full biplot visualisation showing: Coloured prediction grid — blue class 0, red class 1 probability regions. Training data points — coloured confusion category (TP = red, TN = blue, FP = purple, FN = orange). Variable axes — biplot arrows showing direction feature 2D plane. Decision boundary contour lines.
code: plot_biplotEZ(bl_results) #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 110 #>   Predicted 1    : 33  (30.0 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 77  (70.0 %) #>   Accuracy       : 81 / 110  (73.6 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 22 / 59 / 11 / 18 #> --------------------------

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_project_points() projects data frame Z-space bl_results. Pass result plot_biplotEZ() via points argument overlay test set biplot:
code: test_pts <- bl_project_points(bl_results$test_data, bl_results) plot_biplotEZ(bl_results, points = test_pts) #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 30 #>   Predicted 1    : 15  (50.0 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 15  (50.0 %) #>   Accuracy       : 24 / 30  (80.0 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 9 / 15 / 6 / 0 #> --------------------------

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: visual elements can tuned:
code: plot_biplotEZ(   bl_results,   no_grid           = FALSE,        # set TRUE to hide the coloured grid   no_points         = FALSE,        # set TRUE to hide training points   no_contour        = FALSE,        # set TRUE to hide the boundary line   new_title         = "My GLM Boundary",   cex_z             = 0.8,          # training point size   label_dir         = "Hor",        # "Hor" or "Rad"   label_cex         = 1.0,          # variable name (axis label) size   tick_label_cex    = 0.5,          # axis tick label size   ticks_v           = 2L,   label_offset_var  = c(1L, 3L),   label_offset_dist = c(0.8, 1.2),   contour_col       = "darkgreen",   contour_lwd       = 2.0,   contour_lty       = 2L )

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: Phase 2 uses bl_result object Phase 1 perform interpretability analyses across set observations. Phase 2 functions take bl_results first argument.

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_find_boundary() finds nearest decision boundary point observation dataset. row searches pre-computed boundary contour Z-space, inverse-projects closest boundary point back X-space, returns change required variable cross boundary. search always constrains counterfactuals training data range (train_ranges) — observations outside training feature envelope considered feasible counterfactuals.  data argument accepts labelled data frame feature columns training data. search training observations instead: plot.bl_boundary() shows per-variable distances observation counterfactual:
code: # Find boundary for all test observations bl_bnd <- bl_find_boundary(bl_results, data = bl_results$test_data) print(bl_bnd) #> <bl_boundary> #>   Observations   : 30 #>   Boundaries used: 2 #>   Dist Z (mean)  : 0.9647 #>   Dist X (mean)  : 1.1395 #>   B_pred range   : [0.4990, 0.5010] # Overlay counterfactuals on the biplot plot_biplotEZ(bl_results, points = test_pts, boundary = bl_bnd) #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 30 #>   Predicted 1    : 15  (50.0 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 15  (50.0 %) #>   Accuracy       : 24 / 30  (80.0 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 9 / 15 / 6 / 0 #> -------------------------- bl_bnd_train <- bl_find_boundary(bl_results, data = bl_results$train_data) plot(bl_bnd)               # jitter plot (default) #>  #> Robustness (total distance): 38.72 #>  #> Per-variable totals (descending): #>  Sepal.Width Sepal.Length Petal.Length  Petal.Width  #>        27.43         5.49         4.21         1.60 plot(bl_bnd, type = "box") # boxplot #>  #> Robustness (total distance): 38.72 #>  #> Per-variable totals (descending): #>  Sepal.Width Sepal.Length Petal.Length  Petal.Width  #>        27.43         5.49         4.21         1.60

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_robustness() quantifies much variable contributes distance observation counterfactual. Variables larger contributions important crossing boundary. Note: plot(bl_bnd) already prints overall robustness scalar per-variable totals console. Call bl_robustness() separately need access values programmatically — example, compare robustness across models.
code: bl_rob <- bl_robustness(bl_bnd) print(bl_rob) #> $sum_of_distance #> Sepal.Length  Sepal.Width Petal.Length  Petal.Width  #>     5.486750    27.433144     4.205957     1.596877  #>  #> $robustness #> [1] 38.72273

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_surrogate() assigns training observation biplot region (based position relative decision boundary contours) fits simple rule-based surrogate model predicts class region membership. gives interpretable, region-level explanation classifier partitions feature space. Observations fall outside training convex hull receive surrogate_pred = NA excluded surrogate accuracy calculation.
code: bl_sur <- bl_surrogate(bl_results) print(bl_sur) #> <bl_surrogate> #>   Observations        : 110 #>   Surrogate regions   : 2 #>   Accuracy vs model   : 0.8636 #>   Accuracy vs labels  : 0.7091 # Visualise the surrogate regions plot(bl_sur) #> Surrogate accuracy vs model : 0.8636 #> Surrogate accuracy vs labels: 0.7091

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: set_filters() constrains counterfactual search clinically practically plausible variable directions. step optional — omit constraints needed. Valid constraint types: "decrease" — counterfactual value must ≤ observed. "increase" — counterfactual value must ≥ observed. "fixed" — search allows ±0.5 observed value; always reverts original observed value sparse counterfactual. c(min, max) — counterfactual must lie within absolute range.
code: # Filters are illustrated here but not applied in the local search below, # because the constraints that are clinically meaningful depend on the # specific dataset and target. In interactive use, pass flt to # bl_find_local_cf() via the set_filters argument. flt <- set_filters(   tgt,   Petal.Length = "decrease",   # only consider reductions   Sepal.Length = "fixed"       # treat as non-actionable )  print(flt) #> -- bl_filters -- #>   Petal.Length         : decrease #>   Sepal.Length         : fixed

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_find_local_cf() searches nearest decision boundary point relative target observation testing max_pairs eigenvector pairs. pair : Rotates biplot target lies projection plane. Builds local prediction grid rotated Z-space. Applies train_ranges (feasibility) set_filters (actionability, supplied). Finds nearest opposing-class boundary point. pair overall minimum Z-space distance selected. Note: Z-space convex hull polygon applied local search — rotation invalidates original polygon coordinates. train_ranges set_filters constrain search.
code: # Unconstrained search — finds the nearest boundary point across all # eigenvector pairs. Pass set_filters = flt to add actionability # constraints in interactive use. bl_local <- bl_find_local_cf(   bl_result = bl_results,   bl_target = tgt ) #>   Pair 1/6: eigenvectors (1, 2) ... #>     Distance: 1.3450 #>   Pair 2/6: eigenvectors (1, 3) ... #>     Distance: 0.9525 #>   Pair 3/6: eigenvectors (2, 3) ... #>     Distance: 0.7035 #>   Pair 4/6: eigenvectors (1, 4) ... #>     Distance: 0.3751 #>   Pair 5/6: eigenvectors (2, 4) ... #>     Distance: 0.2086 #>   Pair 6/6: eigenvectors (3, 4) ... #>     Distance: 0.2066 #> Best pair: (3, 4) | Distance: 0.2066 print(bl_local) #> -- bl_local_result -- #>   Solution found : TRUE #>   Best pair      : (3, 4) #>   Distance (Z)   : 0.2066 #>   Boundary pred  : 0.4990 #>  #>   Target: #> -- bl_target -- #>   Row ID         : 11 #>   Predicted prob : 0.8260 #>   Predicted class: 1 #>   Feature values : #> Sepal.Length  Sepal.Width Petal.Length  Petal.Width  #>          5.5          2.3          4.0          1.3  #>  #>   All Z-distances by pair: #>   pair  distance #>  (1,2) 1.3449591 #>  (1,3) 0.9524980 #>  (2,3) 0.7034917 #>  (1,4) 0.3751115 #>  (2,4) 0.2085512 #>  (3,4) 0.2066313 # Local biplot: target (filled circle) + counterfactual (grey cross) # Default: training points hidden. Pass no_points = FALSE to show them. plot(bl_local) #>  #> --- Local CF summary --- #>   Target         : 11 #>   Pred prob      : 0.8260  (class 1) #>   Best pair      : (3, 4) #>   Distance (Z)   : 0.2066 #>   Boundary pred  : 0.4990 #> ------------------------ plot(bl_local, no_points = FALSE)   # also show training data #>  #> --- Local CF summary --- #>   Target         : 11 #>   Pred prob      : 0.8260  (class 1) #>   Best pair      : (3, 4) #>   Distance (Z)   : 0.2066 #>   Boundary pred  : 0.4990 #> ------------------------

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_shapley() decomposes path target counterfactual per-variable contributions using exact Shapley formula (p ≤ 14 variables). variable classified : Supports (dark blue) — moving direction helps cross boundary. Contradicts (grey) — moving direction works crossing boundary. Y-axis labels show variable: observed_value → required_change, rounded 3 decimal places match Shapley contribution bar labels.
code: bl_shapley_values <- bl_shapley(bl_local) print(bl_shapley_values) #> -- bl_shapley -- #>   Row ID         : 11 #>   Pred prob      : 0.8260 #>   Pred class     : 1 #>   Boundary pred  : 0.4990 #>  #>   Shapley table: #>      varnames pred_data data_to_boundary shapley_cause  Contribute #>   Petal.Width       1.3          -0.2073        0.1298 Contradicts #>  Petal.Length       4.0          -0.0036       -0.0011    Supports #>  Sepal.Length       5.5           0.3351       -0.0164    Supports #>   Sepal.Width       2.3           0.7619       -0.4393    Supports plot(bl_shapley_values)

title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: bl_find_sparse_cf() creates parsimonious counterfactual : Reverting Contradicts variables observed values. Reverting variables marked "fixed" set_filters() observed values. Optionally rounding Supports variable changes specified resolution (round_to). Re-scoring result model. solution_valid = TRUE means sparse counterfactual still crosses decision boundary. FALSE, try relaxing actionability filters increasing round_to.  round_to resolution-based (rounds nearest multiple). round_to = NULL keeps exact CF values:
code: bl_sparse <- bl_find_sparse_cf(bl_shapley_values, round_to = NULL)  # print() shows three prediction values to verify the class flip: #   Observed  — model score for the original data point #   Full CF   — model score at the full counterfactual #   Sparse CF — model score for the sparse (zeroed) counterfactual print(bl_sparse) #> -- bl_sparse_result -- #>   Solution valid : TRUE #>  #>   Predictions: #>     Observed     : 0.8260  (class 1) #>     Full CF      : 0.4990 #>     Sparse CF    : 0.3240 #>  #>   Variable summary: #>      variable x_obs    B_x x_sparse used_in_sparse #>   Petal.Width   1.3 1.0927   1.3000          FALSE #>  Petal.Length   4.0 3.9964   3.9964           TRUE #>  Sepal.Length   5.5 5.8351   5.8351           TRUE #>   Sepal.Width   2.3 3.0619   3.0619           TRUE # Local biplot with both CFs overlaid: #   Grey cross   = full counterfactual (all variables changed) #   Green cross  = sparse CF (valid — crosses boundary) #   Yellow cross = sparse CF (invalid — does not cross boundary) # To also show training data: plot(bl_sparse, no_points = FALSE) plot(bl_sparse) #>  #> --- Sparse CF summary --- #>   Target         : 11 #>   Observed pred  : 0.8260  (class 1) #>   Full CF pred   : 0.4990 #>   Sparse CF pred : 0.3240 #>   Solution valid : TRUE #> ------------------------- bl_find_sparse_cf(bl_shapley_values, round_to = 0.5)   # nearest 0.5 bl_find_sparse_cf(bl_shapley_values, round_to = 1)      # nearest integer

what: Alternative workflow: bring your own data and model
title: Getting Started with Boundary Logic: Phases 1–3
text: following example shows complete alternative workflow user already pre-split, pre-coded data wants fit model full control hyperparameters.
code: library(boundarylogic)  # Step 1: wrap pre-split data (class column already 0/1) bl_dat <- bl_wrap_data(   train_data   = my_train_df,   # columns: features + "class"   test_data    = my_test_df,   target_class = "versicolor" )  # Step 2: filter outliers bl_filt <- bl_filter_outliers(   bl_data       = bl_dat,   hull_fraction = 0.9,   verbose       = TRUE )  # Step 3: fit your own model on the filtered training data my_svm <- e1071::svm(   class ~ .,   data        = bl_filt$train_data,   probability = TRUE,   type        = "C-classification",   kernel      = "radial",   cost        = 10,   gamma       = 0.5 )  # Wrap it as a bl_model bl_mod <- bl_wrap_model(   model      = my_svm,   model_type = "SVM",   var_names  = bl_filt$var_names,   train_data = bl_filt$train_data ) print(bl_mod)  # Steps 4–6: single call bl_results <- bl_build_result(bl_filt, bl_mod, method = "PCA")  # Visualise plot_biplotEZ(bl_results, new_title = "Custom SVM Boundary")

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: vignette walks complete three-phase Boundary Logic workflow using Pima Indians Diabetes dataset — real-world clinical dataset 768 observations, 8 numeric predictors, binary outcome (diabetic / non-diabetic). Pima dataset demonstrates features arise simple iris example: Non-linear decision surface — handled Generalised Additive Model (GAM) using spline terms, wrapped via bl_wrap_model(). Clinically motivated actionability constraints — variables (Glucose) can influenced treatment, others (Age, Pregnancies) . External patient analysis — predicting explaining outcome new patient training set. workflow follows three phases: Phase 1: Data preparation, model fitting, CVA biplot construction. Phase 2: Global interpretation — distance boundary, per-variable importance, surrogate model. Phase 3: Local interpretation single patient — local counterfactual, Shapley attribution, sparse counterfactual.

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
code: library(boundarylogic) library(dplyr) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: dataset 8 numeric features binary Outcome column (1 = diabetic, 0 = non-diabetic). fitting model, one data quality step applied: Filter implausible zero values — Insulin SkinThickness physiologically zero; rows removed. bl_prepare_data() converts Outcome column class (already 0/1 target_class = NULL) creates reproducible 80/20 train/test split. check: Features — eight clinical measurement columns. Train rows / Test rows — approximately 80/20 filtered dataset. Class balance — check classes represented training set.
code: pima_csv <- system.file("extdata", "pima_diabetes.csv",                         package = "boundarylogic")  pima_raw <- read.csv(pima_csv)  pima_raw <- pima_raw %>%   filter(Insulin > 0, SkinThickness > 0)  bl_dat <- bl_prepare_data(   data           = pima_raw,   class_col      = "Outcome",   target_class   = NULL,       # already 0/1   train_fraction = 0.8,   seed           = 121L )  print(bl_dat) #> <bl_data> #>   Features   : 8 (Pregnancies, Glucose, BloodPressure, SkinThickness, Insulin, BMI, DiabetesPedigreeFunction, Age) #>   Target     : pre-coded 0/1 #>   Train rows : 315  |  Test rows : 79 table(bl_dat$train_data$class) #>  #>   0   1  #> 209 106

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: bl_filter_outliers() removes training observations fall outside convex hull enclosing specified fraction data. hull_fraction = 0.9, outermost 10 % training points removed model fitting.
code: bl_filt <- bl_filter_outliers(bl_dat, hull_fraction = 0.9) #> Hull fraction: 0.90 | Retained: 295 (93.7%) | Removed: 20 (6.3%) print(bl_filt) #> <bl_filter_result> #>   Hull fraction : 0.90 #>   Retained      : 295 (93.7%) #>   Removed       : 20 (6.3%)

what: Step 3 — Fit a GAM and wrap it
title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: Pima outcome non-linear respect predictors. Generalised Additive Model (GAM) spline terms good choice : flexible enough capture non-linear decision surface remaining interpretable. mgcv::gam() fitted directly (giving full control formula spline settings). fitted object registered bl_model via bl_wrap_model() custom predict_fn. model_type = "custom"?mgcv::gam() one built-parsnip-backed types. Supplying predict_fn tells package exactly call predict() model object. function signature must function(model, new_data) returning numeric probability vector.
code: gam_formula <- class ~ s(Glucose) + s(BloodPressure) +   s(Pregnancies) + s(SkinThickness) + Insulin +   s(BMI) + DiabetesPedigreeFunction + Age  custom_gam <- mgcv::gam(   formula = gam_formula,   data    = bl_filt$train_data,   family  = binomial(link = "logit") )  bl_mod <- bl_wrap_model(   model      = custom_gam,   model_type = "custom",   var_names  = bl_filt$var_names,   predict_fn = function(m, new_data) {     as.numeric(mgcv::predict.gam(m, newdata = new_data, type = "response"))   },   train_data = bl_filt$train_data )  print(bl_mod) #> <bl_model> #>   Model type    : custom #>   Cutoff        : 0.5 #>   Train Accuracy: 0.7898  |  Train Gini: 0.7566 #>  #> --- Model summary --- #>            Length Class  Mode     #> model      59     gam    list     #> predict_fn  1     -none- function #> ---------------------

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: bl_build_result() combines projection (Step 4), prediction grid (Step 5), assembly (Step 6) single call. CVA projection used . CVA (Canonical Variate Analysis) maximises -class separation relative within-class spread, makes decision boundary clearly visible 2D biplot plane datasets well-separated binary outcome. rounding = 2L sets wider contour band (cutoff ± 0.01, .e. contour levels 0.49 / 0.51), works well smooth, gradual decision surfaces like produced GAM:
code: bl_results <- bl_build_result(   bl_data  = bl_filt,   bl_model = bl_mod,   method   = "CVA",   title    = "Pima diabetes — GAM, CVA biplot",   rounding = 2L )  print(bl_results) #> <bl_result> #>   Model type   : custom #>   Method       : CVA #>   Features     : 8 (Pregnancies, Glucose, BloodPressure, SkinThickness, Insulin, BMI, DiabetesPedigreeFunction, Age) #>   Train rows   : 295  |  Test rows: 79 #>   Cutoff       : 0.5 #>   Accuracy     : 0.7898  |  Gini: 0.7566 #>   Grid size    : 200 x 200 #>   Hull fraction: 1.00 #>   Created      : 2026-03-25 06:21:45 summary(bl_results) #> <bl_result> #>   Model type   : custom #>   Method       : CVA #>   Features     : 8 (Pregnancies, Glucose, BloodPressure, SkinThickness, Insulin, BMI, DiabetesPedigreeFunction, Age) #>   Train rows   : 295  |  Test rows: 79 #>   Cutoff       : 0.5 #>   Accuracy     : 0.7898  |  Gini: 0.7566 #>   Grid size    : 200 x 200 #>   Hull fraction: 1.00 #>   Created      : 2026-03-25 06:21:45 #>  #> --- Model performance (training data) --- #> Confusion matrix (train): #>          Actual #> Predicted   0   1 #>         0 177  43 #>         1  19  56 #>  #> Grid probability range: [0.0050, 0.9860] #> Contour lines found  : 2 #> Polygon filter       : applied (fraction = 1.00) #> Note: CVA forced standardise = FALSE.

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: biplot shows CVA prediction surface (blue = non-diabetic, red = diabetic probability regions), training observations coloured confusion category, decision boundary contour:  Overlay test observations:
code: plot_biplotEZ(bl_results) #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 295 #>   Predicted 1    : 75  (25.4 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 220  (74.6 %) #>   Accuracy       : 233 / 295  (79.0 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 56 / 177 / 19 / 43 #> -------------------------- test_pts <- bl_project_points(bl_results$test_data, bl_results) plot_biplotEZ(bl_results, points = test_pts) #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 79 #>   Predicted 1    : 26  (32.9 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 53  (67.1 %) #>   Accuracy       : 61 / 79  (77.2 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 16 / 45 / 10 / 8 #> --------------------------

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: Phase 2 uses bl_results object interpret model population level: finding nearest boundary point every observation, measuring variable-level distances, fitting surrogate model.

what: Step 7 — Find nearest boundary point: bl_find_boundary()
title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: bl_find_boundary() searches decision boundary contour observation back-projects nearest boundary point X-space. result summarises far observation decision boundary variables drive distance. search constrained train_ranges — counterfactuals must lie within range training feature envelope. Overlay boundary arrows biplot (arrow runs observation nearest boundary point):
code: bl_bnd <- bl_find_boundary(bl_results) print(bl_bnd) #> <bl_boundary> #>   Observations   : 79 #>   Boundaries used: 2 #>   Dist Z (mean)  : 0.1133 #>   Dist X (mean)  : 2.7521 #>   B_pred range   : [0.4900, 0.5100] plot_biplotEZ(bl_results, points = test_pts, boundary = bl_bnd) #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 79 #>   Predicted 1    : 26  (32.9 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 53  (67.1 %) #>   Accuracy       : 61 / 79  (77.2 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 16 / 45 / 10 / 8 #> --------------------------

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: plot(bl_bnd) decomposes observation’s distance boundary signed, standardised contribution per variable. Variables sorted least important (bottom top). Y-axis: variable : total absolute standardised distance (importance proxy). X-axis: signed standardised distance — positive means observation boundary variable’s direction. Colours: TP = red, TN = blue, FP = purple, FN = orange. overall robustness scalar per-variable totals printed console.   Note: plot(bl_bnd) already prints overall robustness scalar per-variable totals console. Call bl_robustness(bl_bnd) separately need access values programmatically — example, compare robustness across models.
code: plot(bl_bnd) #>  #> Robustness (total distance): 326.34 #>  #> Per-variable totals (descending): #>                  Glucose                  Insulin                      BMI  #>                    68.22                    52.48                    51.55  #>            SkinThickness                      Age              Pregnancies  #>                    49.64                    41.87                    25.78  #>            BloodPressure DiabetesPedigreeFunction  #>                    19.85                    16.97 plot(bl_bnd, type = "boxplot") #>  #> Robustness (total distance): 326.34 #>  #> Per-variable totals (descending): #>                  Glucose                  Insulin                      BMI  #>                    68.22                    52.48                    51.55  #>            SkinThickness                      Age              Pregnancies  #>                    49.64                    41.87                    25.78  #>            BloodPressure DiabetesPedigreeFunction  #>                    19.85                    16.97

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: bl_surrogate() assigns training observation class based solely position Z-space relative hull-clipped surrogate contour lines. provides purely spatial approximation classifier confined training hull. Observations project outside training hull receive surrogate_pred = NA. Two accuracy measures reported: accuracy_vs_model — agreement fitted GAM’s predictions. accuracy_vs_labels — accuracy true class labels.
code: bl_surr <- bl_surrogate(bl_results) print(bl_surr) #> <bl_surrogate> #>   Observations        : 295 #>   Surrogate regions   : 2 #>   Accuracy vs model   : 0.9797 #>   Accuracy vs labels  : 0.7831 plot(bl_surr) #> Surrogate accuracy vs model : 0.9797 #> Surrogate accuracy vs labels: 0.7831

what: Step 10 — Inspect predictions and select a target
title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: bl_predict() scores every test observation returns tidy data frame. Inspect choose patient want explain, wrap row bl_target object using bl_select_target().

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: clinical context, variables can changed given patient. set_filters() encodes constraints: "decrease" — counterfactual value must ≤ observed (e.g., lowering Glucose via diet/medication realistic intervention). "fixed" — variable actionable; search allows ±0.5 observed value, sparse counterfactual always reverts original observed value. "increase" — counterfactual value must ≥ observed. c(min, max) — counterfactual must lie within absolute range. patient, Glucose primary clinical lever (can reduced), Age Pregnancies biological facts changed:
code: flt <- set_filters(   tgt,   Glucose     = "decrease",   # reducing Glucose is the clinical lever   Age         = "fixed",      # Age is not actionable   Pregnancies = "fixed"       # Pregnancies is not actionable )  print(flt) #> -- bl_filters -- #>   Glucose              : decrease #>   Age                  : fixed #>   Pregnancies          : fixed

text: bl_find_local_cf() searches nearest decision boundary point relative target patient testing multiple eigenvector pairs. pair : Rotates biplot target lies projection plane. Builds local prediction grid rotated Z-space. Applies train_ranges (feasibility) actionability filters Step 12. Finds nearest opposing-class boundary point. pair overall minimum Z-space distance selected. Note: Z-space convex hull polygon applied local search — rotation invalidates original polygon coordinates. train_ranges set_filters constrain search.

title: Boundary Logic with Real Clinical Data: The Pima Diabetes Dataset
text: bl_find_sparse_cf() creates parsimonious counterfactual reverting Contradicts variables observed values, reverting variables marked "fixed" set_filters(). Supports variables take counterfactual values. solution_valid = TRUE confirms sparse counterfactual still crosses decision boundary (predicted probability changes class 1 class 0).
code: bl_sparse <- bl_find_sparse_cf(bl_shapley_values, round_to = NULL)  # print() shows three predictions to verify the class flip: #   Observed  — model score for the original patient #   Full CF   — model score at the full counterfactual #   Sparse CF — model score for the sparse (zeroed) counterfactual print(bl_sparse) #> -- bl_sparse_result -- #>   Solution valid : TRUE #>  #>   Predictions: #>     Observed     : 0.7580  (class 1) #>     Full CF      : 0.4900 #>     Sparse CF    : 0.4940 #>  #>   Variable summary: #>                  variable   x_obs      B_x x_sparse used_in_sparse #>                   Insulin 175.000 146.3515 175.0000          FALSE #>             BloodPressure  72.000  69.7465  69.7465           TRUE #>                       Age  51.000  50.7225  51.0000           TRUE #>               Pregnancies   5.000   4.6992   5.0000           TRUE #>  DiabetesPedigreeFunction   0.587   0.5509   0.5509           TRUE #>                       BMI  25.800  22.6012  22.6012           TRUE #>                   Glucose 166.000 157.5975 157.5975           TRUE #>             SkinThickness  19.000  13.7952  13.7952           TRUE # Grey cross   = full counterfactual (all variables changed) # Green cross  = sparse CF (valid — crosses boundary) # Yellow cross = sparse CF (invalid — does not cross boundary) plot(bl_sparse) #>  #> --- Sparse CF summary --- #>   Target         : 1 #>   Observed pred  : 0.7580  (class 1) #>   Full CF pred   : 0.4900 #>   Sparse CF pred : 0.4940 #>   Solution valid : TRUE #> -------------------------

title: Biplot-Based Decision Boundary Interpretability for Classification Models
text: Biplot-based decision boundary interpretability binary classification models boundarylogic R package provides post hoc explanations binary classification models. projects high-dimensional data 2D PCA CVA biplot space, approximates model’s decision boundary reduced space, identifies counterfactual observations — nearest points flip predicted class.

title: Biplot-Based Decision Boundary Interpretability for Classification Models
text: decision boundary classifier (GLM, GAM, GBM, SVM, XGB, …) approximated 2D biplot plane, making visually interpretable regardless number features. Counterfactuals identified nearest boundary points biplot space, inverse-projected back original feature space. Local interpretations (per-observation: need change?) global interpretations (variable importance distance boundary, surrogate models) supported. Shapley values decompose path observation counterfactual per-variable contributions. Actionability constraints let restrict counterfactual searches clinically practically plausible directions. 2D boundary approximation — package designed communicate clearly still providing actionable insight.

title: Biplot-Based Decision Boundary Interpretability for Classification Models
text: package organised around three phases. Phase 1 outputs stored single bl_result object acts reproducible anchor Phases 2 3.
code: Phase 1 ─ Build the pipeline   bl_prepare_data()  →  bl_filter_outliers()  →  bl_fit_model() / bl_wrap_model()   →  bl_build_result()  →  plot_biplotEZ()  Phase 2 ─ Global interpretation (population level)   bl_find_boundary()  →  plot(bl_bnd)  →  bl_surrogate()  Phase 3 ─ Local interpretation (single observation)   bl_predict()  →  bl_select_target()  →  set_filters()  →  bl_find_local_cf()   →  bl_shapley()  →  bl_find_sparse_cf()

title: Biplot-Based Decision Boundary Interpretability for Classification Models
code: library(boundarylogic)  # Phase 1: prepare data, fit model, build biplot bl_dat <- bl_prepare_data(   data           = iris,   class_col      = "Species",   target_class   = "versicolor",   # 1 = versicolor, 0 = all others   train_fraction = 0.8,   seed           = 42L )  bl_filt <- bl_filter_outliers(bl_dat, hull_fraction = 0.91)  bl_mod <- bl_fit_model(   train_data = bl_filt$train_data,   var_names  = bl_filt$var_names,   model_type = "GLM" )  bl_results <- bl_build_result(   bl_data  = bl_filt,   bl_model = bl_mod,   method   = "PCA",   rounding = 3L )  plot_biplotEZ(bl_results)  # Overlay test data test_pts <- bl_project_points(bl_results$test_data, bl_results) plot_biplotEZ(bl_results, points = test_pts) # Phase 2: global counterfactual search bl_bnd <- bl_find_boundary(bl_results) plot(bl_bnd)               # jitter plot — per-variable distances + robustness plot(bl_bnd, type = "box") # boxplot by confusion category  bl_sur <- bl_surrogate(bl_results) plot(bl_sur) # Phase 3: local interpretation of one observation pred_summary <- bl_predict(bl_results) tdp <- which(pred_summary$pred_class == 1)[1]   # first predicted class-1 row tgt <- bl_select_target(bl_results, target = tdp)  bl_local <- bl_find_local_cf(bl_results, tgt) plot(bl_local)  bl_shapley_values <- bl_shapley(bl_local) plot(bl_shapley_values)  bl_sparse <- bl_find_sparse_cf(bl_shapley_values, round_to = NULL) print(bl_sparse) plot(bl_sparse)

title: Biplot-Based Decision Boundary Interpretability for Classification Models
text: Use bl_wrap_model() fitted model externally — example, using custom hyperparameters model type natively supported.
code: # Example: custom GAM with spline terms (Pima diabetes dataset) library(mgcv)  custom_gam <- mgcv::gam(   class ~ s(Glucose) + s(BMI) + s(BloodPressure) + Insulin + Age,   data   = bl_filt$train_data,   family = binomial(link = "logit") )  bl_mod <- bl_wrap_model(   model      = custom_gam,   model_type = "custom",   var_names  = bl_filt$var_names,   predict_fn = function(m, new_data) {     as.numeric(mgcv::predict.gam(m, newdata = new_data, type = "response"))   },   train_data = bl_filt$train_data )  bl_results <- bl_build_result(bl_filt, bl_mod, method = "CVA", rounding = 2L) plot_biplotEZ(bl_results)

code: flt <- set_filters(   tgt,   Glucose     = "decrease",    # can only be reduced   Age         = "fixed",       # not actionable   Pregnancies = "fixed"        # not actionable )  bl_local <- bl_find_local_cf(bl_results, tgt, set_filters = flt)

title: Biplot-Based Decision Boundary Interpretability for Classification Models
text: cutoff = 0.5 : non-default cutoff values accepted give valid boundary results current methodology. 2D boundary approximation: boundarylogic designed exploratory interpretability, exact boundary reconstruction. 2D projection captures dominant structure decision surface represent every dimension high-dimensional model. rounding controls contour band width : rounding parameter bl_build_result() / bl_build_grid() determines tightly boundary contour extracted (rounding = 2L → band ±0.01; rounding = 3L → band ±0.001). model predictions always stored 3 decimal places regardless setting. Local search apply convex hull: bl_find_local_cf() rotates biplot plane, invalidates original hull polygon. local search constrained train_ranges set_filters.

title: Assemble Phase 1 artifacts into a single result object — bl_assemble
code: # Step 1: prepare data bl_dat  <- bl_prepare_data(datasets::iris, class_col = "Species",                             target_class = "versicolor")  # Step 2 (optional): iterative polygon filter bl_filt <- bl_filter_outliers(bl_dat, hull_fraction = 0.9)  # Step 3: fit final model (on filtered data) bl_mod  <- bl_fit_model(bl_filt$train_data, bl_filt$var_names,                          model_type = "GLM")  # Step 4: build projection bl_proj <- bl_build_projection(bl_filt$train_data, bl_filt$var_names,                                 method = "PCA")  # Step 5: build prediction grid bl_grid <- bl_build_grid(bl_filt$train_data, bl_proj, bl_mod)  # Step 6: assemble result  <- bl_assemble(bl_dat, bl_filt, bl_mod, bl_proj, bl_grid)

title: Assemble Phase 1 artifacts into a single result object — bl_assemble
text: train_data Post-filter training data frame. test_data Test data frame (filtered training variable ranges). var_names Feature column names. num_vars Number features. model Fitted model object. model_type Character; model family. cutoff Decision threshold. rounding Rounding precision. V Loading matrix (p x p). tV Inverse V (p x p). X_center Column means. X_sd Column standard deviations. method "PCA" "CVA". standardise Effective standardise flag. proj_dims Integer vector length 2; eigenvector indices used 2D projection plane. biplot_obj biplotEZ object Phase 2 plotting. polygon Convex hull polygon (SpatialPolygons NULL). hull_fraction Hull fraction used, NULL. biplot_grid full bl_grid list. accuracy Training accuracy. gini Training Gini coefficient. call R call created object. created_at POSIXct creation timestamp.

what: Build the biplot prediction grid — bl_build_grid
title: Build the biplot prediction grid — bl_build_grid
text: Creates m x m grid points 2D biplot Z-space, back-projects point original feature space, scores every grid point fitted model, extracts contour lines approximate decision boundary. Also computes convex hull polygon bounding training data.

title: Build the biplot prediction grid — bl_build_grid
code: bl_build_grid(   train_data,   bl_projection,   bl_model,   m = 200L,   cutoff = 0.5,   b_margin = NULL,   rounding = 2L,   polygon = NULL,   outlie = 0.9,   calc_hull = FALSE,   calc_ct_in_hull = FALSE )

title: Build the biplot prediction grid — bl_build_grid
text: train_data Data frame feature columns (post-filter training data). Used project points Z-space define hull polygon polygon NULL. bl_projection "bl_projection" object bl_build_projection(). bl_model "bl_model" object bl_fit_model(). m Integer; grid resolution (m x m points per side). Default 200L. Increase finer boundary resolution cost computation time. cutoff Numeric; decision threshold. Default 0.5. b_margin Numeric; half-width band around cutoff used extract contour lines. Default NULL, resolves 1 / (10^rounding). rounding Integer; floor-based rounding precision grid scores. Default 2. polygon sp::SpatialPolygons object use hull boundary. NULL (default), new polygon computed train_data using aplpack::plothulls() fraction = outlie. outlie Numeric (0, 1]; hull fraction used polygon NULL. Default 0.9. calc_hull Logical; TRUE, grid points outside polygon removed returned grid. Default FALSE. calc_ct_in_hull Logical; TRUE, outside-polygon grid cells set cutoff contour extraction, contours confined hull interior. Default FALSE.

title: Build the biplot prediction grid — bl_build_grid
text: list class "bl_grid" components: Zgrid Numeric matrix (m² x 2); grid coordinates Z-space. Xgrid Data frame (m² x p); grid back-projected X-space. grid_prob Numeric vector (length m²); model scores per grid point, 0, 1. Z_train Numeric matrix (n x 2); training data projected Z-space. col_value Character vector (length m²); colour per grid point blue-white-red scale. min_val Numeric; lower bound square plot region. max_val Numeric; upper bound square plot region. polygon sp::SpatialPolygons convex hull. ct List contour line objects returned grDevices::contourLines(). xseq Numeric vector (length m); x-axis grid sequence. yseq Numeric vector (length m); y-axis grid sequence. pred_use Numeric 0/1 vector (length n); predicted class training point. pred_col Character vector (length n); colour label per training point based confusion category. class Numeric 0/1 vector (length n); true class training point.

code: bl_build_projection(   train_data,   var_names,   method = "PCA",   standardise = TRUE,   proj_dims = c(1L, 2L),   bl_model = NULL,   cva_classes = NULL,   title = "" )

title: Build a PCA or CVA projection matrix for boundary logic analysis — bl_build_projection
text: train_data Data frame feature columns "class" column (numeric 0/1). class column used derive confusion labels CVA bl_model supplied. var_names Character vector feature column names. method "PCA" "CVA". Default "PCA". standardise Logical; whether standardise features PCA. Ignored (forced FALSE) method = "CVA". Default TRUE. proj_dims Integer vector length 2; eigenvector indices use biplot plane. Default c(1L, 2L). bl_model "bl_model" object bl_fit_model() bl_wrap_model(). method = "CVA" cva_classes NULL, bl_model used compute four-class confusion labels (TP, TN, FP, FN) passed cva_classes. Ignored PCA. cva_classes Factor class labels, one per row train_data. supplied, takes precedence bl_model CVA. Use .factor(train_data$class) simple alternative model fitted. title Character string biplot title. Default "".

title: Build a PCA or CVA projection matrix for boundary logic analysis — bl_build_projection
text: list class "bl_projection" components: V Numeric matrix (p x p); loading / eigenvector matrix. tV Numeric matrix (p x p); solve(V). X_center Numeric vector (length p); column means feature matrix. X_sd Numeric vector (length p); column standard deviations. method "PCA" "CVA". standardise Logical; effective standardise flag CVA override. proj_dims Integer vector; eigenvector indices used. biplot_obj biplotEZ S3 object; used plotting Phase 2.

title: Build a PCA or CVA projection matrix for boundary logic analysis — bl_build_projection
text: CVA always forces standardise = FALSE internally, regardless value supplied user. CVA uses within-class covariance structure already accounts scale. effective standardise value recorded returned object.

title: Build a PCA or CVA projection matrix for boundary logic analysis — bl_build_projection
text: PCA (method = "PCA"): suitable data; separates observations overall variance. Use class structure well-defined feature space. CVA (method = "CVA"): maximises -class separation relative within-class spread. CVA technically requires least 3 distinct class levels, biplotEZ supports 2-class data (results may degenerate). bl_model supplied, four confusion categories (TP, TN, FP, FN) used class labels, satisfying 3-class requirement. simpler alternative, pass cva_classes = .factor(train_data$class) directly (2 levels ).

title: Build a PCA or CVA projection matrix for boundary logic analysis — bl_build_projection
code: bl_dat  <- bl_prepare_data(datasets::iris,                             class_col    = "Species",                             target_class = "versicolor") # PCA projection bl_proj <- bl_build_projection(bl_dat$train_data,                                 bl_dat$var_names,                                 method = "PCA")  # CVA with bl_model — confusion labels (TP/TN/FP/FN) used automatically bl_mod <- bl_fit_model(bl_dat$train_data, bl_dat$var_names) bl_proj_cva <- bl_build_projection(   bl_dat$train_data,   bl_dat$var_names,   method    = "CVA",   proj_dims = c(1L, 2L),   bl_model  = bl_mod )  # CVA alternative: pass binary class factor directly (2 levels only) bl_proj_cva2 <- bl_build_projection(   bl_dat$train_data,   bl_dat$var_names,   method      = "CVA",   proj_dims   = c(1L, 2L),   cva_classes = as.factor(bl_dat$train_data$class) ) #> Warning: CVA works best with at least 3 class levels. Only 2 level(s) found. Consider supplying bl_model to use TP/TN/FP/FN labels (4 levels), or use method = 'PCA'. #> Warning: The dimension of the canonical space < dim.biplot sample.opt method used for additional dimension(s).

what: Remove outliers from training data using a convex hull polygon filter — bl_filter_outliers
title: Remove outliers from training data using a convex hull polygon filter — bl_filter_outliers
text: Projects training data lightweight 2-PC space, builds convex hull enclosing hull_fraction points, removes observations outside hull, reports number percentage rows retained removed.

title: Remove outliers from training data using a convex hull polygon filter — bl_filter_outliers
code: bl_filter_outliers(bl_data, hull_fraction = 0.9, verbose = TRUE)

title: Remove outliers from training data using a convex hull polygon filter — bl_filter_outliers

title: Wrap an externally fitted model for use in boundary logic analysis — bl_wrap_model
code: # Fit a GLM with custom settings, then wrap it bl_dat <- bl_prepare_data(datasets::iris, class_col = "Species",                            target_class = "versicolor") my_glm <- glm(class ~ ., data = bl_dat$train_data,               family = binomial(link = "logit")) bl_mod <- bl_wrap_model(my_glm, model_type = "GLM",                          var_names  = bl_dat$var_names,                          train_data = bl_dat$train_data)  # Custom model with predict_fn my_fn  <- function(m, new_data) predict(m, new_data, type = "response") bl_mod <- bl_wrap_model(my_glm, model_type = "custom",                          var_names  = bl_dat$var_names,                          predict_fn = my_fn,                          train_data = bl_dat$train_data)

title: Plot the Phase 1 biplot with decision boundary — plot_biplotEZ

title: Plot the Phase 1 biplot with decision boundary — plot_biplotEZ
code: bl_dat  <- bl_prepare_data(datasets::iris, class_col = "Species",                             target_class = "versicolor") bl_mod  <- bl_fit_model(bl_dat$train_data, bl_dat$var_names) bl_proj <- bl_build_projection(bl_dat$train_data, bl_dat$var_names) bl_grid <- bl_build_grid(bl_dat$train_data, bl_proj, bl_mod, m = 100L) result  <- bl_assemble(bl_dat, bl_model = bl_mod,                         bl_projection = bl_proj, bl_grid = bl_grid)  plot_biplotEZ(result) #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 120 #>   Predicted 1    : 32  (26.7 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 88  (73.3 %) #>   Accuracy       : 85 / 120  (70.8 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 19 / 66 / 13 / 22 #> --------------------------  # Highlight a target observation target <- unlist(result$test_data[1, result$var_names]) plot_biplotEZ(result, target_point = target, target_label = 1)  #>  #> --- Prediction summary --- #>   Points plotted : 120 #>   Predicted 1    : 32  (26.7 %) #>   Predicted 0    : 88  (73.3 %) #>   Accuracy       : 85 / 120  (70.8 %) #>   TP / TN / FP / FN : 19 / 66 / 13 / 22 #> --------------------------
